title: Flawed Topological Sort of a Graph with Multiple Connected Components

statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: Graph represented as an adjacency list, stored in a file with the vertices first, separated by a newline, followed by the adjacency list with edges, each separated by a space. No multiple edges between the same vertices. The graph may have multiple connected components.
statement.outputFormat: List of topological orderings for each strongly connected component.
statement.notes: The graph may have disconnected components. The input file contains the vertices first, followed by a newline, then the adjacency list. Each line represents a set of edges connected to a vertex.

examples.explanation: Topological order for strongly connected components: {{3, 0, 1, 2}} and {{4}}, {{3}}

hints.text: Topological sorting is not the same as a topological traversal. A topological traversal gives you the order in which to visit the vertices.

editorial.title: Correct Approach to Topological Sort
editorial.intuition: Sort topologically by visiting all vertices in reverse DFS order, and then perform DFS on each vertex before it is visited.

testCases.input: First line count, second line elements (stdin match)
